steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage from Defoe's 1704 book contrasts sermons ("sound of words spoken to the ear," limited by memory) with printed books ("a record," able to convey "its contents for ages to come"). The main idea is about the comparative staying power of written versus spoken words.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Words committed to print have a greater permanence than messages that are merely spoken aloud.: Directly captures the central contrast: spoken words fade with memory, printed words endure for ages. Every line of the passage supports this. ✓
  • Choice B — People are less likely to forget a message when they hear it spoken aloud than they are when they read it in print.: Reverses the passage's claim. Defoe says the opposite — spoken words depend on "the strength of memory" while printed books can "renew its acquaintance with his memory" at will. ✗
  • Choice C — Unless a spoken message is delivered by an expert, it can be safely ignored.: Introduces expertise not mentioned anywhere in the passage. The passage says nothing about who delivers a sermon or whether qualifications affect its value. ✗
  • Choice D — Most authors have little hope of being remembered well past their lifetimes.: Contradicts the passage. Defoe's point is that books "convey contents for ages to come" even "when the author is forgotten" — the message outlasts the author, not that authors lack hope. ✗
That is the correct answer.
